elements.sent-2411-001: The classification of distracted drivers is pivotal for ensuring safe driving.
elements.sent-2411-002: Previous studies demonstrated the effectiveness of neural networks in automatically predicting driver distraction, fatigue, and potential hazards.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, recent research has uncovered a significant loss of accuracy in these models when applied to samples acquired under conditions that differ from the training data.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we introduce a robust model designed to withstand changes in camera position within the vehicle.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our Driver Behavior Monitoring Network (DBMNet) relies on a lightweight backbone and integrates a disentanglement module to discard camera view information from features, coupled with contrastive learning to enhance the encoding of various driver actions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments conducted on the daytime and nighttime subsets of the 100-Driver dataset validate the effectiveness of our approach with an increment on average of 9\% in Top-1 accuracy in comparison with the state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-007: In addition, cross-dataset and cross-camera experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets, namely AUCDD-V1, EZZ2021 and SFD, demonstrate the superior generalization capability of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-008: As of 2019, road traffic accidents are the leading killer of children and youth aged 5 to 29 years and are the 12th leading cause of death when all ages are considered \cite{world2023global .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Traffic accidents pose a growing problem and are projected to rank as the seventh leading cause of global mortality by 2030.\footnote{\url{https://www.who.int/data/gho/data/themes/topics/topic-details/GHO/road-traffic-mortality (accessed: \lastaccess).}}
elements.sent-2411-010: According to statistics from the European Road Safety Observatory (ERSO), 5 to 25\% of traffic accidents in Europe stem from driver distraction.\footnote{\url{https://road-safety.transport.ec.europa.eu/system/files/2021-07/ersosynthesis2018-driverdistraction-summary.pdf (accessed: \lastaccess).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The ERSO defines distracted driving as ``the diversion of attention away from activities critical for safe driving toward a competing activity, which may result in insufficient or no attention to activities critical for safe driving''.
elements.sent-2411-012: Commonly observed distractions include smartphone usage, eating, or conversing with passengers, all of which heighten the risk of accidents and should be rigorously avoided.
elements.sent-2411-013: Detecting these distracting behaviors through automatic in-vehicle computational systems enables early intervention to avoid distraction-related accidents.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this context, automatic Driver Monitoring Systems (DMS) have emerged as a novel Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) technology to mitigate car accidents \cite{choi2016driver .}
elements.sent-2411-015: DMSs exploit machine and deep learning techniques and are trained on datasets acquired in both real and simulated scenarios, often with varying protocols and experimental setups \cite{eraqi2019driver,wang2023100,Hu_Driver2023,bianco2023platform .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Significant differences among these setups include the interior configuration of the vehicle, the position and posture of the driver and the camera placement.
elements.sent-2411-017: Consequently, models trained on specific datasets exhibit poor generalization to different data \cite{beheraLatentBodyPoseGuided2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: To deploy these models on non-production embedded systems in various vehicles while maintaining performance, the models must be robust to prior variations.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, they must generalize to new conditions without increasing computational complexity, considering the limited capacity of the target devices.
elements.sent-2411-020: This paper addresses the challenge of cross-camera distracted driver classification by introducing the Driver Behavior Monitoring Network (DBMNet).
elements.sent-2411-021: This model is based on the use of a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for encoding RGB images to facilitate its deployment on commercial off-the-shelf devices.
elements.sent-2411-022: To make the model robust across different camera views, we propose a feature disentanglement module.
elements.sent-2411-023: This module separates action-relevant features from view-relevant features, allowing the proposed model to learn high quality features that are invariant to camera and view variations.
elements.sent-2411-024: This is achieved by a weighted combination of the view queries and the feature vector determined by the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-025: The weights are calculated on the basis of the similarity between the test sample and the training views.
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, we employ a supervised contrastive learning approach with triplet loss to enhance the learning of disentangled features.
elements.sent-2411-027: While cross-entropy loss theoretically allows the model to discern similarities within categories and differences between them, it does not explicitly guarantee this outcome.
elements.sent-2411-028: In contrast, triplet loss explicitly compels the model to identify similar features for samples within the same category and distinguish features for samples from different categories.
elements.sent-2411-029: In our model, one triplet loss brings representations of different actions captured by the same camera closer together while separating them from identical actions captured by the same camera.
elements.sent-2411-030: This improves the model's ability to discriminate between different views and enhances the effectiveness of the disentanglement module.
elements.sent-2411-031: Another triplet loss brings representations of identical actions captured by different cameras closer together while separating different actions captured by the same camera.
elements.sent-2411-032: This aids in the categorization of driver actions.
elements.sent-2411-033: The proposed work is unique because no previous approach has explicitly addressed modeling invariance in experimental configurations.
elements.sent-2411-034: We adopt a leave-one-camera-out approach, which mirrors the realistic scenario where abundant data from various cameras is available for training, and experimentation occurs under diverse conditions.
elements.sent-2411-035: We demonstrate that our novel network and learning procedure alleviates the degradation in cross-camera distracted driver classification on the very recent 100-Driver dataset \cite{wang2023100 .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Experiments on the AUCDD-V1 \cite{eraqi2019driver , EZZ2021 \cite{ezzouhri2021robust , and SFD \cite{statefarm2020sfd datasets demonstrate the superior cross-dataset and cross-camera performance of our approach, highlighting its generalization capability.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-038: A novel disentanglement module that makes features invariant to camera views, thereby enhancing driver action classification.
elements.sent-2411-039: Two triplet losses to aid in learning disentangled representations.
elements.sent-2411-040: A model that maintains low computational complexity while outperforming more advanced methods on four benchmark datasets.
elements.sent-2411-041: Feature analysis and ablation studies provide valuable insights on feature disentanglement and the impact of different design choices.
elements.sent-2411-042: Large-scale image recognition networks have proven effective in recognizing distracted driving.
elements.sent-2411-043: Abouelnaga \etal \cite{Abouelnaga_Realtime2018 employed a genetic-weighted ensemble of four CNNs, focusing on facial and hand features.}
elements.sent-2411-044: Baheti \etal \cite{Baheti_Detection2018 optimized VGG16, reducing parameters and introducing $L_2$ regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Koay \etal \cite{Koay_Convolutional2021 favored CNNs over Vision Transformers and proposed the OWIPA approach with pose estimation.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Shaout \etal \cite{Shaout_Embedded2021 used SqueezeNet for real-time distraction detection.}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, in all these works cross-dataset performance was overlooked.
elements.sent-2411-048: Specially designed models like MobileVGG with 2.2M parameters \cite{Baheti_Computationally2020 advanced real-time detection.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Nguyen \etal \cite{Duy-LinhNguyen_Driver2022 developed a lightweight CNN with adaptive feature map extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Li \etal \cite{Li_Driver2022 proposed OLCMNet, an accurate lightweight network with an Octave-Like Convolution Mixed (OLCM) block.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Liu \etal \cite{Liu_Extremely2023 used knowledge distillation and NAS, achieving high accuracy with 0.42M parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Mittal \etal \cite{Mittal_CATCapsNet2023 introduced CAT-CapsNet with impressive accuracies.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Despite the reported improvements, fewer parameters may limit learning capabilities, especially in cross-dataset scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-054: To mitigate the detrimental effects of background noise on recognition accuracy, Leekha \etal \cite{leekhaAreYouPaying2019 proposed the use of an iterative graph-based foreground segmentation algorithm to separate the driver from the background, while Yang \etal \cite{Xing_Driver2019 employed Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) for body extraction.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Qin \etal \cite{Qin_Distracted2022 introduced D-HCNN with 0.76M parameters, focusing on HOG features.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Dey \etal \cite{deyContextdrivenDetectionDistracted2021 used object detection for multiple ROIs, enhancing generalization.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Behera \etal \cite{Behera_Deep2022 proposed a contextual modeling approach that integrates posture and semantic context.}
elements.sent-2411-058: They validated their approach using a self-annotated SFD test set and provided the first cross-validation results for the SFD dataset.
elements.sent-2411-059: Wang \etal \cite{wangDataAugmentationApproach2021 applied Fast-RCNN for DOA extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Keypoint-based approaches, like Li \etal \cite{liNovelSpatialTemporalGraph2019 and Bera \etal \cite{Bera_Attend2021 , emphasized topological information.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: While effective, these methods demand extensive pre-processing, impacting real-time performance.
elements.sent-2411-062: Duan \etal \cite{duan2023enhancing proposed S-Softmax classifier and a dynamic 2D supervision matrix for improving cross-dataset performance.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Researchers simplified training strategies for enhanced generalization.
elements.sent-2411-064: Masood \etal \cite{Masood_Detecting2020 used pretrained-weight VGG architectures for reduced training time.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Li \etal \cite{Li_Learning2022 introduced Multi-Teacher Knowledge Distillation for lightweight models.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Peng \etal \cite{Peng_TransDARC2022 employed Few-Shot Learning for feature calibration.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Contrastive and unsupervised methods, like Hu \etal \cite{Hu_Driver2023 and Li \etal \cite{Li_New2022 , focused on improved training strategies.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Compared to previous research, this paper tackles the issue of distracted drivers by enhancing model robustness without increasing computational demands.
elements.sent-2411-069: Notably, this is the first work to conduct comprehensive experiments using the leave-one-camera-out strategy, accurately reflecting real-world scenarios where the recognition model is trained under certain conditions and tested under different ones.
elements.sent-2411-070: This rigorous setup ensures our findings are both practical and applicable to real-world conditions.
elements.section-2411-003: The proposed Driver Behavior Monitoring Network
elements.sent-2411-071: Image analysis-based DMSs encounter a significant challenge due to the variability in vehicle interior configurations, resulting in disparate camera viewpoints \cite{Peng_TransDARC2022,wang2023100 .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Conventional supervised deep learning methods, often employing cross-entropy loss, struggle to develop robust representations capable of generalizing across different views.
elements.sent-2411-073: In these varied perspectives, where driver actions are observed, it becomes crucial to design supervised techniques that mitigate the drop in effectiveness when handling data acquired under conditions that differ from the training set.
elements.sent-2411-074: Moreover, deploying these DMSs on devices with limited computational capacity necessitates the use of efficient and lightweight models, ensuring real-time predictions.
elements.sent-2411-075: Hence, robust solutions must be sought, incorporating deep learning models that respond to previous requirements.
elements.sent-2411-076: Given the factors mentioned above, we present the Driver Behavior Monitoring Network (DBMNet), illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-077: This method encodes an RGB image by leveraging the lightweight CNN architecture detailed in \cite{wang2023100 as its backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The proposed feature disentanglement module aims to prioritize action encoding over camera-view information.
elements.sent-2411-079: This is achieved by linearly combining view queries with features estimated by the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-080: The resulting disentangled features are then forwarded to a linear layer for action classification.
elements.sent-2411-081: The model outputs two probability distributions: one for the driver's action categories and another for the viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-082: The viewpoint distribution is used solely as an auxiliary task for the feature disentanglement module.
elements.sent-2411-083: The model is optimized through cross-entropy losses combined with triplet losses to assist in the learning of view-invariant action features.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.9\linewidth]{inference.pdf} (a) \includegraphics[width=.9\linewidth]{training.pdf} (b) \caption{The DBMNet pipeline. At inference time (a), the backbone encodes an RGB image into a feature vector $\mathbf{f}$. The feature disentanglement module then refines these features to discard view-related information while retaining action-related details using view queries, $Q$, together with the probability distribution over the views. The resulting features $\mathbf{\hat{f}}$ are mapped into a driver action by the action classifier. During training (b), alongside the cross-entropy loss for action and view prediction (respectively, $\mathcal{L}_{ace}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{vce}$), we employ the triplet loss ($\mathcal{L}_{ac}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{vc}$). This triplet loss helps to learn distinct action and view representations by processing three input images: an anchor image $a$, an image $sv$ depicting the same view but different action as $a$, and an image $sa$ with the same action but from a different view as $a$.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-084: Our model consists of a GhostNet-v1.0 \cite{han2020ghostnet as the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Given an input image $\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{C\times H\times W}$, the backbone $f_b$ outputs a feature vector $\mathbf{f = f_{b (\mathbf{X ) \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times D}$ as output, where $D$ is the number of features.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: The output of the backbone is passed to the feature disentanglement module.
elements.sent-2411-087: This module consists of $V$ learnable view queries $\mathbf{Q \in \mathbb{R ^{D \times V}$, where $V$ represents the number of views for the training samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Through our learning process, we determine, for each view, which features are most relevant for characterizing the driver actions.
elements.sent-2411-089: The disentangled feature vector is obtained as a weighted combination of the view queries with the feature vector $\mathbf{f}$, determined by the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-090: The weighting, denoted as $\mathbf{w}$, is computed based on which view or views the test sample most closely resembles.
elements.sent-2411-091: In practice, estimating the disentangled features is carried out as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{f} = w \times f, with \quad w = p_v \cdot Q,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{p _v$ is the probability distribution over $V$ views that is estimated by the view classifier.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\hat{f} = w \times f, with \quad w = p_v \cdot Q,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-092: The model consists of two classifiers, namely the Action classifier for categorizing the action performed by the driver and the View classifier for the estimation of the camera viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Action classifier takes as input the disentangled feature vector $\hat{\mathbf{f }$ and outputs the probability distribution over the $A$ action classes, $\mathbf{p _a \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times A}$, as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{p}_a = (\mathbf{W}_a \hat{\mathbf{f}}) + \mathbf{b}_a,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{W _a \in \mathbb{R ^{D\times A}$ and $\mathbf{b _a \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times A}$ are the weights and bias of the fully-connected head for action classification.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Instead, the View classifier processes the feature vector $\mathbf{f}$ produced directly from the backbone to produce the probability distribution, $\mathbf{p _v$, on the $V$ views as follows: \begin{equation}\mathbf{p}_v = (\mathbf{W}_v \mathbf{f}) + \mathbf{b}_v.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-095: In the previous equations, $\mathbf{W _v \in \mathbb{R ^{D\times V}$ and $\mathbf{b _v \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times V}$ are the weights and bias of the fully-connected layer for view classification.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: During training, our model processes batches of image triplets with an approach similar to that of Siddiqui \etal \cite{siddiqui2024dvanet .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Each triplet comprises an anchor image, denoted as $a$, which portrays a driver's action from a specific viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-098: Another image in the triplet, referred to as $sv$ (same view), displays a different action captured from the identical viewpoint as $a$.
elements.sent-2411-099: Finally, we include an image of the same action as $a$, but captured from a distinct viewpoint, denoted as $sa$ (same action).
elements.sent-2411-100: We construct these triplets to provide both positive and negative samples for our supervised contrastive learning, which will be presented in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-101: By supplying both positive and negative samples, the model can develop a comprehensive understanding of the discriminative characteristics within each action or viewpoint category through sample comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-102: This methodology diverges from the learning-by-classification approach, wherein the loss function solely penalizes classification errors without explicitly promoting intraclass compactness and interclass separation \cite{schroff2015facenet .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Our DBMNet is trained through a composite loss function that is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{ace} + \mathcal{L}_{vce} + \mathcal{L}_{ac} + \mathcal{L}_{vc}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: In the previous loss, there are two cross-entropy losses mainly dedicated to the optimization of linear layers for view ($\mathcal{L _{vce}$) and action ($\mathcal{L _{ace}$) categorization.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Cross-entropy losses are only applied to the anchor image in this way: \begin{equation}L_{vce} = -\sum_{m=1}^Vy^{(m)}_vlog(p_v^{(m)}), L_{ace} = -\sum_{m=1}^Ay^{(m)}_alog(p_a^{(m)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-106: The previous two cross-entropy losses are complemented by two opposite triplet losses.
elements.sent-2411-107: Both losses use the same triplet construction previously defined.
elements.sent-2411-108: The difference lies in the role assumed by $sv$ ed $sa$ in each loss.
elements.sent-2411-109: In the action triplet loss (Eq. \ref{eq:loss-ac ) the objective is to learn features for action encoding that are invariant regardless of the viewpoint.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Hence, $sa$ serves as the positive sample, whereas $sv$ serves as the negative sample.
elements.sent-2411-111: This choice has the purpose of bringing closer the representations of $sa$ and $a$ which have the same action.
elements.sent-2411-112: Conversely, the distance between the representations of $a$ and $sv$ that depict a different action from the same point of view is instead maximized: \begin{equation}\label{eq:loss-ac} \mathcal{L}_{ac} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} [\delta + \mathcal{D}(\mathbf{\hat{f}}_a, \mathbf{\hat{f}}_{sa})-\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{\hat{f}}_a, \mathbf{\hat{f}}_{sv})],\end{equation} where $\hat{\mathbf{f _a$, $\hat{\mathbf{f _{sa}$, and $\hat{\mathbf{f _{sv}$ denote the disentangled feature outputs from the $a$, $sa$, and $sv$ images, respectively.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: It is expected that the learned disentangled features exhibit view-invariance, as they are encouraged to be as similar as possible when derived from two different images depicting the same action regardless of the viewpoint from which they were acquired.
elements.sent-2411-114: For the view triplet loss, positive and negative samples are reversed: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{vc} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} [\delta + \mathcal{D}(\mathbf{f}_a, \mathbf{f}_{sv})-\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{f}_a, \mathbf{f}_{sa})].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-115: In this way, the feature disentanglement module, particularly the view queries, learns to generate similar features for images acquired from the same viewpoint, regardless of the action being performed.
elements.sent-2411-116: Since our primary goal is to enhance multi-view driver action recognition, training our model to distinguish action-relevant features from view-relevant features in this manner provides cleaner and more accurate action representations that are robust to changes in the camera viewpoint.
elements.sent-2411-117: In this section, we present the datasets used in the experiments, describe the experimental setup, and discuss the results of our extensive experimentation.
elements.sent-2411-119: The 100-Driver dataset comprises 470,000 images illustrating 100 drivers engaged in 22 distinct actions during driving sessions.
elements.sent-2411-120: These actions include 21 distracted behaviors and one instance of normal driving (see Table \ref{tab:action-list for the list of all actions).}

elements.sent-2411-122: Within each vehicle, four cameras were strategically placed - on the left front, right front, and right side of the drivers.
elements.sent-2411-124: Firstly, having a dataset with multiple cameras aids in developing more robust models that can account for variations in camera perspectives, thus evaluating the models' generalization across different cameras.
elements.sent-2411-125: Secondly, the multi-camera dataset offers an opportunity to enhance system performance by considering the content captured from multiple camera angles.
elements.sent-2411-126: Figure \ref{fig:samples reports samples from the four cameras in both day and night times.}

elements.sent-2411-141: Our experiments are performed on a computer featuring an Intel i7-8700 CPU @3.20GHz and an NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1070 Ti.

elements.sent-2411-149: We then use random rotation in the range [-30$^\circ$, 30$^\circ$], random perspective, random color transformations in terms of contrast, saturation and hue, Gaussian blur, and random erasing \cite{zhong2020random for data augmentation.}

elements.sent-2411-154: In this section we present the results detailing the contribution of the key components within the proposed model.
elements.sent-2411-155: Specifically, we compare three different efficient backbones and we assess the contribution of contrastive learning, the influence of the disentanglement module, and finally, the effect of their combined integration.
elements.sent-2411-156: Table \ref{tab:architectures reports the comparison of the three model architectures in terms of number of parameters and number of floating point operations per second (FLOPS).}
elements.sent-2411-157: The GhostNet-v1.0 model has 5.2 million parameters and 0.16 GFlops, which indicates a moderate computational complexity with a relatively higher number of parameters compared to the other models.
elements.sent-2411-158: On the other hand, the MobileNetV3-S model, demonstrates a significantly lower number of parameters at 2.5 million and the lowest computational complexity with 0.06 GFlops.
elements.sent-2411-159: Finally, the SqueezeNet-v1.1 model features the smallest number of parameters at 1.2 million but has the highest computational complexity among the three, with 0.35 GFlops.

elements.sent-2411-160: The results reported in Table \ref{tab:backbone-results reveal notable performance differences among the considered backbones in the leave-one-camera-out experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-161: GhostNet consistently demonstrates superior performance across most daytime and nighttime scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-162: For instance, it achieves the highest Top-1 accuracy of 62.51\% and Top-5 accuracy of 93.75\% when evaluating D2.
elements.sent-2411-163: This suggests that GhostNet is particularly effective in handling variations across different camera configurations.
elements.sent-2411-164: MobileNetV3 also performs well, especially during nighttime acquisitions, where it shows competitive results in some scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-165: For example, it achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 33.18\% for N1 and 47.75\% for N2, indicating its robustness in lower light conditions.
elements.sent-2411-166: However, its overall performance remains slightly lower than GhostNet's across most evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-167: SqueezeNet, on the other hand, consistently underperforms compared to GhostNet and MobileNetV3.

elements.sent-2411-231: In this section, we conduct an analysis of features to support our claim of disentangled features.
elements.sent-2411-232: To this end, we qualitatively evaluate how the features before and after disentanglement module separate in the embedding space and quantitatively estimate the capacity of discriminating viewpoints before and after feature disentanglement.
elements.sent-2411-233: Practically, we extract feature vectors before and after the View disentanglement module, namely $\mathbf{f}$ and $\mathbf{\hat{f }$, for both training and validation images.}
elements.sent-2411-234: We chose validation images because the camera view categories overlap with those of the training set, unlike those of the test set.

elements.sent-2411-236: The colors represent different driver action classes.
elements.sent-2411-237: As depicted, the \(\mathbf{f}\)-features separate into small, distinct clusters, even for the same action class, likely due to the actions being captured by different cameras.
elements.sent-2411-238: In contrast, the \(\mathbf{\hat{f }\)-features form larger clusters that more effectively distinguish between different actions regardless of the camera used, thereby demonstrating the effectiveness of the feature disentanglement module.}

elements.sent-2411-246: Driver Monitoring Systems (DMSs) need to be robust to changes in vehicle configuration and camera positions.
elements.sent-2411-247: In this paper, we introduced DBMNet, a novel network designed for distracted driver classification, ensuring consistent performance regardless of camera placement.
elements.sent-2411-248: Given that DMSs operate on embedded devices with constrained computational resources, our model exploits GhostNet, a lightweight CNN, as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-249: We improved the invariance to the camera view of the representation using a feature disentanglement module.
elements.sent-2411-250: In addition, we employed a supervised contrastive learning approach to increase consistency between intra-class representations and differentiate inter-class representations.
elements.sent-2411-251: Experimental results on the 100-Driver dataset demonstrated the effectiveness of the proposed model with an increment on average of 9\% in Top-1 accuracy with respect to state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-252: Through the analysis of features both before and after the feature disentanglement module, we determined that the objective of discarding view information is accomplished.
elements.sent-2411-254: Given the inherent complexity of the task, the accuracy results obtained are modest, indicating a need for further refinement to enhance the generalization capacity of our DBMNet.
elements.sent-2411-255: This requirement is particularly pronounced due to the low accuracy observed in the ``Normal driving'' class, which often gets confused with other distraction classes.
elements.sent-2411-256: Additionally, the inference time on our computational setup stands at 1.15 × $10^{-2}$ seconds, which remains relatively high for ensuring timely detection of driver distraction, hindering the implementation of effective safety systems.
elements.sent-2411-257: Considering these identified limitations, as part of future endeavors, we aim to explore lighter backbones beyond GhostNet utilized in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-258: This exploration aims to minimize the inference time while maintaining performance.
elements.sent-2411-259: Furthermore, we plan to investigate few-shot learning methods to calibrate the model to novel viewpoints, leveraging a limited number of training samples.
elements.sent-2411-260: These strategies are pivotal for addressing the current constraints and advancing the effectiveness of DMSs.

elements.sent-2411-306: A first group of contrastive loss-based papers uses supervised contrastive loss to align representations of the same class (i.e., action) and separate those of different classes.
elements.sent-2411-307: For example, Kopuklu \etal~\cite{kopuklu2021driver proposed a contrastive learning approach to learn a metric to differentiate normal driving from anomalous driving and introduced a new Driver Anomaly Detection (DAD) dataset with front and top drivers views in both infrared and depth modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Koay \etal~\cite{koay2023contrastive proposed a deep contrastive learning approach exploiting multi-view and multi-modal data of DAD dataset to identify normal and anomalous driving in an open set manner, fusing together the results of different views and modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-309: More often, instead, they rely on self-supervised contrastive learning to learn generalizable embeddings without explicit labels for every behavior and typically use temporal or multimodal consistency, or augmentations, to form positive and negative pairs.
elements.sent-2411-310: Yang \etal~\cite{Yang_Quantitative2023 proposed a weakly supervised contrastive learning framework, in which distracted behaviors are identified based on their distances from the normal driving representation set.}
elements.sent-2411-311: Hu \etal~\cite{Hu_Driver2023 extended the previous approach by proposing a novel clustering supervised contrastive loss to optimize the distribution of the extracted representation vectors, so that normal representations are better clustered while abnormal ones are separated.}
elements.sent-2411-312: Khan \etal~\cite{khan2022supervised presented a new supervised contrastive loss function along with considerations for including projection head and refining labels to improve the detection of anomalous driving behaviors from videos.}
elements.sent-2411-313: Triplet loss-based papers instead construct triplets (anchor, positive, negative) to ensure that feature embeddings of similar driver actions (i.e., same class) are closer and those of different actions are farther apart.
elements.sent-2411-314: Okon \etal~\cite{okon2017detecting used the triplet loss with an offline selection of hard triplets to finetune a neural network pre-trained with classification loss.}
elements.sent-2411-315: Liu \etal~\cite{liu2021tml used the triplet loss in a multi-task learning strategy to force the networks to explore global information by multiple tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-316: The negative sample in each triplet is generated by randomly shuffling the local regions of each given input.

elements.sent-2411-319: This cross-camera variability introduces a critical challenge: conventional supervised deep learning methods trained on a fixed viewpoint, often employing cross-entropy loss, struggle to learn robust representations capable of generalizing across different views and, therefore, their performance degrade when they are applied to unseen views.
elements.sent-2411-320: Most existing methods overlook this issue, focusing instead on maximizing accuracy within a fixed viewpoint -- an unrealistic assumption for actual deployment.
elements.sent-2411-321: Moreover, DMSs must run efficiently on embedded hardware, where computational and memory resources are limited.
elements.sent-2411-322: Prior approaches typically either focus on model complexity reduction or on classification accuracy improvement, but rarely address both cross-camera robustness and resource efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-323: In this work, we address both challenges jointly.
elements.sent-2411-324: We propose a learning strategy that explicitly disentangles driver action features from viewpoint-dependent cues, enabling the model to generalize across camera perspectives while maintaining a lightweight structure suitable for real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-325: We introduce the feature disentanglement module, which is based on two complementary triplet losses to guide the network to learn robust, semantically meaningful representations.
elements.sent-2411-326: The feature disentanglement module uses the first triplet loss to train an action-independent view classifier and exploits it as a hyper-network to remove of camera view information from learned features.
elements.sent-2411-327: The second triplet loss is used to further reinforce viewpoint consistency across actions.
elements.sent-2411-328: Our goal is not only to improve classification accuracy, but to ensure transferability and reliability in real deployment scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=.9\linewidth]{inference.pdf} (a) \includegraphics[width=.92\linewidth]{training.pdf} (b) \caption{The DBMNet pipeline. At inference time (a), the backbone encodes an RGB image into a feature vector $\mathbf{f}$. The feature disentanglement module then refines these features to discard view-related information while retaining action-related details using view queries, $Q$, together with the probability distribution over the views. The resulting features $\mathbf{\hat{f}}$ are mapped into a driver action by the action classifier. During training (b), alongside the cross-entropy loss for action and view prediction (respectively, $\mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{ace}}}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{vce}}}$), we employ the triplet loss ($\mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{ac}}}$ and $\mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{vc}}}$). This triplet loss helps to learn distinct action and view representations by processing three input images: an anchor image $a$, an image $sv$ depicting the same view but different action as $a$, and an image $sa$ with the same action but from a different view as $a$.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-329: Given an input image $\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{C\times H\times W}$, the backbone $f_b$ outputs a feature vector $\mathbf{f = f_b(\mathbf{X ) \in \mathbb{R ^{1\times D}$ as output, where $D$ is the number of features.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-330: In practice, estimating the disentangled features is carried out as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{f} = w \times f, with \quad w = p_v \cdot Q,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{p _v$ is the probability distribution over $V$ views estimated by the view classifier, \ADD{which therefore acts as a hyper-network \cite{ha2017hypernetworks,chauhan2024brief .}}}
elements.sent-2411-331: The inner structure of this module might resemble memory banks, which are typically a learnable, or fixed, set for feature vectors or embeddings that the network can query and update: the idea of memory banks is to provide a module that can store feature representations so the model can retrieve and reuse them.
elements.sent-2411-332: Our feature disentanglement module instead learns a set of view queries that are independently multiplied for the feature vector coming from the backbone and linearly combined using as weights the probability distribution over the views that is estimated by the view classifier.
elements.sent-2411-333: As such, it also shares some similarities with the attention mechanism \cite{schug2024attention .}
elements.sent-2411-334: During training, our model processes batches of image triplets with an approach similar to that of Siddiqui \etal~\cite{siddiqui2024dvanet .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Our DBMNet is trained through a composite loss function that is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:loss} \mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{ace}}} + \mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{vce}}} + \ADD{\lambda_{\rm{ac}}} \mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{ac}}} + \ADD{\lambda_{\rm{vc}}}\mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{vc}}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-336: In the previous loss, there are two cross-entropy losses mainly dedicated to the optimization of linear layers for view ($\mathcal{L _{\ADD{\rm{vce }$) and action ($\mathcal{L _{\ADD{\rm{ace }$) categorization.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-337: Cross-entropy losses are only applied to the anchor image in this way: \begin{equation}L_{\ADD{\rm{vce}}} = -\sum_{m=1}^Vy^{(m)}_vlog(p_v^{(m)}), L_{\ADD{\rm{ace}}} = -\sum_{m=1}^Ay^{(m)}_alog(p_a^{(m)}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-338: The previous two cross-entropy losses are complemented by two opposing triplet losses, \ADD{whose contributions are controlled by the hyperparameters $\lambda_{\rm{ac }$ and $\lambda_{\rm{vc }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-339: Both losses rely on the triplet structure previously introduced.
elements.sent-2411-340: The difference lies in the role assumed by $sv$ and $sa$ in each loss.
elements.sent-2411-341: This choice has the purpose of bringing closer the representations of $sa$ and $a$, which have the same action.
elements.sent-2411-342: For the view triplet loss, the positive and negative samples are reversed: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\ADD{\rm{vc}}} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} [\delta + \mathcal{D}(\mathbf{f}_a, \mathbf{f}_{sv})-\mathcal{D}(\mathbf{f}_a, \mathbf{f}_{sa})].\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-394: The proposed DBMNet and the competing methods for distracted driver classification are deployed on the Coral Dev Board 4GB, a commercially available ARM-based Single-Board Computer (SBC) designed specifically for edge AI applications~\cite{coral-board .}
elements.sent-2411-395: This development board integrates a Google Edge TPU coprocessor, a specialized hardware accelerator optimized for efficient execution of deep learning models directly on the device.
elements.sent-2411-396: The Edge TPU is an 8-bit fixed-point coprocessor capable of performing high-speed inference on quantized TensorFlow Lite models with low power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-397: This makes it particularly suitable for real-time, on-device inference tasks, such as monitoring driver distraction.
elements.sent-2411-398: The Coral Dev Board therefore enables compact, energy-efficient, and responsive deployment of the proposed system in embedded automotive environments.
elements.sent-2411-399: All methods are deployed following post-training quantization using full integer quantization.
elements.sent-2411-400: This optimization technique converts both the model weights and activations from 32-bit floating-point to 8-bit fixed-point representations.
elements.sent-2411-401: It requires calibration with a representative dataset to determine optimal quantization parameters for the activations.
elements.sent-2411-402: For this purpose, we use the validation set of the 100-Driver dataset in a leave-one-camera-out configuration.
elements.sent-2411-403: A quantized version of each model is generated for every configuration of daylight data.
elements.sent-2411-404: Due to the limited computational resources of the Coral Dev Board, the DGCCL model could not be successfully deployed and is therefore excluded from this evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-405: Performance is evaluated across five metrics:.
elements.sent-2411-406: Average error represents the classification error obtained across the four folds of the leave-one-camera-out experiment;.
elements.sent-2411-407: Model size refers to the size of the quantized model file (including architecture and weights), reported in megabytes (MB);.
elements.sent-2411-408: Memory usage is the average memory consumption (in MB) during inference, computed over 100 inference passes;.
elements.sent-2411-409: Inference time is the average time (in seconds) required for a single forward pass, again averaged over 100 runs;.
elements.sent-2411-410: Power consumption is estimated by measuring the energy used across 100 inference passes, scaled by the average inference time, and reported in milliampere-hours (mAh).
elements.sent-2411-411: The radar plot reported in Figure \ref{fig:radar-plot highlights DBMNet (ours) as the best-performing model, achieving the lowest average error while maintaining a compact model size, low memory usage, fast inference time, and minimal power consumption -- making it ideal for real-time, embedded applications.}
elements.sent-2411-412: Compared to other methods, DBMNet offers the best trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-413: Duan \etal~and GhostNet are as lightweight and power-efficient as DBMNet but suffers from high error rates.
elements.sent-2411-414: Hu \etal~is the largest and most resource-intensive without offering a corresponding gain in accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-415: OLCMNet fall between these extremes but do not match DBMNet's overall balance.
elements.figure-2411-021: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{radar_plot} \caption{\ADD{Radar plot comparing the proposed DBMNet with competing methods deployed on the Coral Dev Board across five key metrics: average error, model size, memory usage, inference time, and power consumption.}} \label{fig:radar-plot}
elements.sent-2411-416: Extensive experiments on the 100-Driver dataset demonstrated that DBMNet achieves a 9\% improvement in Top-1 accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-417: Overall, the proposed method achieves a 7\% gain in Top-1 accuracy compared to existing approaches.
elements.sent-2411-418: To validate real-world applicability, we deployed DBMNet and competing methods on a Coral Dev Board board.
elements.sent-2411-419: In this embedded setup, DBMNet demonstrated the lowest average error, while maintaining a compact model size, low memory footprint, fast inference time, and minimal power consumption.
elements.sent-2411-420: Due to the complexity of the task, overall accuracy is still modest, especially for ``Normal driving'' class, which is frequently misclassified as a distraction category.
elements.sent-2411-421: Additionally, the inference time of 1.15 × $10^{-2}$ seconds on board is relatively high for real-time safety-critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-422: To address these issues, future work will focus on exploring lighter backbones beyond GhostNet to reduce inference time without sacrificing performance.
elements.sent-2411-423: We also plan to investigate few-shot learning strategies for model calibration on unseen camera viewpoints using limited labeled data.
elements.sent-2411-424: These improvements aim to enhance both the efficieny and adaptability of DBMNet in real-world DMS deployments.
elements.sent-2411-425: This contributes a new perspective to the field by explicitly addressing viewpoint variability—an underexplored but practically crucial challenge.
